name: STEMI — Inferior
identifyBy: ST elevation ≥1mm in 2 or more inferior leads (II, III, aVF)
Reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL (mirror image)
ST elevation greater in lead III than II suggests RCA occlusion
ST elevation greater in lead II than III suggests LCx occlusion
Check right-sided leads (V3R–V6R) — RV infarct present in up to 50%

measurements.stSegment: Elevation II, III, aVF; Depression I, aVL (reciprocal)

management: 12-lead ECG — confirm ≥1mm ST elevation in II, III, aVF
Obtain right-sided ECG (V3R–V6R) — critical to exclude RV infarct
GTN — USE WITH EXTREME CAUTION if RV infarct suspected (will cause profound hypotension)
IV fluid bolus 250–500mL if hypotensive with RV infarct
pearls: GTN is CONTRAINDICATED in RV infarction — the right ventricle is preload-dependent; GTN drops preload and causes circulatory collapse
Bezold-Jarisch reflex: inferior STEMI → vagal surge → bradycardia + hypotension + diaphoresis — treat with atropine and IV fluids
AV blocks (1°, 2° Mobitz I, even 3°) are common in inferior STEMI due to AV node ischaemia — usually transient
Posterior extension (tall R in V1–V2, ST depression) indicates larger territory — higher risk
New complete heart block in inferior STEMI: pace if haemodynamically unstable
